manifest.description: Blueprint-compiled draft dispatch pack for math.found.quantifiers (Phase 1 front-end; DRAFT only)

assets.title: Quantifiers ‚ÄĒ cognitive map
assets.content:
### Core Insight
Bounded quantifiers ‚ÄĒ ‚ąÄx‚ąąA and ‚ąÉx‚ąąA ‚ÄĒ restrict quantification to a named set A rather than a universal domain. The critical asymmetry: bounded ‚ąÄ expands using IMPLICATION (‚Üí), while bounded ‚ąÉ expands using CONJUNCTION (‚ąß). Getting this wrong produces statements with entirely different truth values.

### Conceptual Hierarchy
```
Level 0 (Concrete): Bounded quantifier examples on finite sets; enumerate to verify.
Level 1 (Pictorial): Venn-diagram expansion: A is a subset of the full domain;
                      ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) checks only elements inside circle A.
Level 2 (Abstract): Formal expansion laws; uniqueness quantifier ‚ąÉ!; set-theory bridge.
Level 3 (Transfer): Express A‚äÜB, A‚ą©B, A^c using bounded quantifier notation.
```

### Core Definitions
```
BOUNDED UNIVERSAL QUANTIFIER:
  ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x)  ‚Č°  ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí P(x))
  Read: "For all x in A, P(x)."
  Truth: TRUE iff for every element a ‚ąą A, P(a) is TRUE.
  Vacuously true when A = ‚ąÖ (no elements to check).

BOUNDED EXISTENTIAL QUANTIFIER:
  ‚ąÉx‚ąąA P(x)  ‚Č°  ‚ąÉx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x))
  Read: "There exists x in A such that P(x)."
  Truth: TRUE iff at least one element a ‚ąą A satisfies P(a).
  FALSE when A = ‚ąÖ (no witnesses available).

UNIQUENESS QUANTIFIER:
  ‚ąÉ!x P(x)  ‚Č°  ‚ąÉx(P(x) ‚ąß ‚ąÄy(P(y) ‚Üí y=x))
  Read: "There exists a unique x such that P(x)."
  TRUE iff exactly one element satisfies P.

CONNECTIVE ASYMMETRY ‚ÄĒ WHY ‚Üí FOR ‚ąÄ AND ‚ąß FOR ‚ąÉ:
  ‚ąÄx‚ąąA using ‚ąß: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)) claims EVERY x (in the whole domain) is in A.
    This is almost always false ‚ÄĒ it over-constrains.
  ‚ąÉx‚ąąA using ‚Üí: ‚ąÉx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí P(x)) is trivially true whenever ‚ąÉx‚ąČA
    (False‚ÜíP is True), making the statement vacuously true.
    This is almost always trivially true ‚ÄĒ it under-constrains.
```

---

assets.title: [FOUNDATIONAL]: BOUNDED-UNIVERSAL-WRONG-CONNECTIVE
assets.content:
### MC-1 [FOUNDATIONAL]: BOUNDED-UNIVERSAL-WRONG-CONNECTIVE
**Belief:** ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Č° ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)) ‚ÄĒ uses ‚ąß instead of ‚Üí.
**Origin:** "For all x in A AND P(x)" feels natural; the ‚ąß seems to express "both conditions hold."
**Trigger:** Student writes ‚ąÄn‚ąą‚Ąē(n>0) as ‚ąÄn(n‚ąą‚Ąē ‚ąß n>0). Over domain ‚Ą§: this claims every integer is a natural number ‚ÄĒ FALSE, but the intended statement is TRUE.
**Repair:** ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)) asserts that EVERY object in the universe is in A. This is much stronger (usually false). The correct meaning "all members of A satisfy P" is captured by conditional: if x is in A, then P(x).
**Priority:** FOUNDATIONAL ‚ÄĒ this error corrupts all formal mathematical statements.

assets.title: BOUNDED-EXISTENTIAL-WRONG-CONNECTIVE
assets.content:
### MC-2: BOUNDED-EXISTENTIAL-WRONG-CONNECTIVE
**Belief:** ‚ąÉx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Č° ‚ąÉx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí P(x)) ‚ÄĒ uses ‚Üí instead of ‚ąß.
**Origin:** Mirroring the fix for MC-1 ("bounded quantifiers always use ‚Üí") without distinguishing ‚ąÄ from ‚ąÉ.
**Trigger:** ‚ąÉx‚ąą{2,4}(x is odd): student writes ‚ąÉx(x‚ąą{2,4} ‚Üí x is odd). The expansion is trivially TRUE (take x=3 not in {2,4}: False‚Üíanything is True). The correct statement is FALSE.
**Repair:** For ‚ąÉ, we need to find an x that IS in A AND satisfies P ‚ÄĒ both conditions active simultaneously: ‚ąÉx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)).

assets.title: UNIQUENESS-IS-EXISTENCE
assets.content:
### MC-3: UNIQUENESS-IS-EXISTENCE
**Belief:** ‚ąÉ!x P(x) is just another way to write ‚ąÉx P(x) ‚ÄĒ uniqueness adds no content.
**Origin:** Students read "there exists a unique" as emphasis on existence, not a separate logical condition.
**Trigger:** ‚ąÉ!x(x¬≤=4) over ‚ĄĚ: student says TRUE (there exists such an x). But ‚ąÉ!x(x¬≤=4) is FALSE (both x=2 and x=‚ąí2 satisfy it; two solutions, not one).
**Repair:** ‚ąÉ! is strictly stronger than ‚ąÉ. ‚ąÉx requires at least one. ‚ąÉ!x requires exactly one.

---

assets.title: Concrete Foundation ‚ÄĒ Bounded Quantifiers and Connective Choice
Mastery Gate (P91 Terminal)
assets.content:
### TA-A04: Mastery Gate (P91 Terminal)

```
P77 ‚ÄĒ MULTI-PROBLEM SET (4 problems)

Problem 1:
"Expand each into an unbounded quantifier form, stating the connective used:
(a) ‚ąÄx‚ąą{1,2,3} (x < 5)   over D = ‚Ąē.
(b) ‚ąÉx‚ąą{1,2,3} (x is prime)   over D = ‚Ąē."

Problem 2:
"Determine whether each statement is TRUE or FALSE.
Domain D = ‚Ą§. Set A = {‚ąí3, ‚ąí1, 0, 1, 3}.
(a) ‚ąÄx‚ąąA (x¬≤ ‚Č§ 9)
(b) ‚ąÉx‚ąąA (x¬≤ = 2)
(c) ‚ąÉ!x‚ąąA (x¬≤ = 0)"

Problem 3:
"Negate: ‚ąÄx‚ąą‚Ąē ‚ąÉ!y‚ąą‚Ąē (y = x + 1).
Write the negation in a simplified form (no ¬¨ on a quantifier)."
[¬¨(‚ąÄx‚ąą‚Ąē ‚ąÉ!y‚ąą‚Ąē(y=x+1)) ‚Č° ‚ąÉx‚ąą‚Ąē ¬¨(‚ąÉ!y‚ąą‚Ąē(y=x+1))
‚Č° ‚ąÉx‚ąą‚Ąē (‚ąÄy‚ąą‚Ąē(y‚Č†x+1) ‚ą® ‚ąÉy,z‚ąą‚Ąē(y‚Č†z ‚ąß y=x+1 ‚ąß z=x+1))
Simplified: ‚ąÉx‚ąą‚Ąē where the unique successor doesn't exist or isn't unique.
Over ‚Ąē the original is TRUE; negation is FALSE ‚ÄĒ but the exercise tests the negation law.]

Problem 4:
"True or false: ‚ąÉ!x‚ąą‚ĄĚ (x¬≤ ‚ąí 5x + 6 = 0). Justify."
[x¬≤‚ąí5x+6=(x‚ąí2)(x‚ąí3)=0; x=2 or x=3. Two solutions ‚Üí NOT unique. FALSE.]
```

```
P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE (after P77)
Award 1 point per problem.
Running total before P76: __/4.
```

```
P76 ‚ÄĒ TRANSFER PROBE (cross-link: math.found.set-theory ‚ÄĒ Tier 1)
"Using bounded quantifier notation, express the following set-theory definitions.
Then verify each for the concrete sets A = {2,3,5}, B = {1,2,3,4}, domain D = {1,2,3,4,5}.

(a) Express 'A is a subset of B' (A ‚äÜ B) using ‚ąÄx‚ąąA.
    Verify whether A ‚äÜ B is TRUE or FALSE.

(b) Express 'the intersection A‚ą©B consists of all x in D that are in both A and B'
    using ‚ąÉx‚ąąD with the appropriate conjunction.
    List the elements of A‚ą©B.

(c) Express the complement A^c (relative to D) as a bounded existential or
    bounded universal statement about membership in A. List the elements of A^c.

(d) In one sentence: how does this exercise show that set theory is a specialisation
    of predicate logic with bounded quantifiers?"

[Expected:
(a) ‚ąÄx‚ąąA(x‚ąąB) ‚Č° ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí x‚ąąB). 5‚ąąA but 5‚ąČB, so A‚äĄB ‚ÄĒ FALSE.
(b) A‚ą©B = {x‚ąąD : x‚ąąA ‚ąß x‚ąąB}. Elements: {2,3}.
    Quantified check: ‚ąÄx‚ąąD (x‚ąąA‚ą©B ‚ÜĒ x‚ąąA ‚ąß x‚ąąB).
(c) A^c = D\A = {1,4}. Expressed: {x‚ąąD : x‚ąČA}; or: ‚ąÄx‚ąąD(x‚ąąA^c ‚ÜĒ x‚ąČA).
(d) Set operations (subset, intersection, complement) are shorthand for specific
    bounded quantifier statements over a domain ‚ÄĒ set theory is predicate logic
    applied to membership predicates.]
```

```
P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE (after P76)
Award 1 point for P76.
Running total: __/5.
```

```
P75 ‚ÄĒ MASTERY ASSESSMENT
Pass criterion: 4 out of 5 (‚Ćą0.80 √ó 5‚ĆČ = 4)
```

```
P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE (final)
Record pass/fail status.
```

```
P74 ‚ÄĒ ROUTING DECISION
Score ‚Č• 4/5 ‚Üí MASTERY ACHIEVED ‚Üí P78 COMPLETION
Score < 4/5 ‚Üí NEEDS REVIEW ‚Üí identify lowest-scoring item; activate Protocol B for its misconception
```

```
P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE (routing recorded)
```

```
P78 ‚ÄĒ COMPLETION
"Quantifiers checkpoint complete. You have established:
  ‚ÄĘ Bounded ‚ąÄ: ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Č° ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí P(x)). Connective: IMPLICATION (‚Üí).
  ‚ÄĘ Bounded ‚ąÉ: ‚ąÉx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Č° ‚ąÉx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)). Connective: CONJUNCTION (‚ąß).
  ‚ÄĘ Uniqueness: ‚ąÉ!x P(x) ‚Č° ‚ąÉx(P(x) ‚ąß ‚ąÄy(P(y)‚Üíy=x)). Exactly one, not 'at least one.'
  ‚ÄĘ Set theory encodes bounded quantifiers: A‚äÜB ‚Č° ‚ąÄx‚ąąA(x‚ąąB); A‚ą©B uses ‚ąß.
Next concepts: math.found.proof (every proof uses ‚ąÄ/‚ąÉ strategies),
math.found.set-theory (full set algebra in bounded-quantifier language)."
```

---


## Component 5 ‚ÄĒ Protocol B (Misconception Repair Chains)

### Repair Chain B-1: BOUNDED-UNIVERSAL-WRONG-CONNECTIVE (MC-1 FOUNDATIONAL)

```
P64 ‚ÄĒ CONCEPTUAL SHIFT
"BEFORE: ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Č° ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)).
 AFTER:  ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Č° ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí P(x)).

Why ‚ąß fails: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)) says EVERY x in the WHOLE domain is in A AND satisfies P.
But A is just a subset ‚ÄĒ most x's are NOT in A. The conjunction fails for them.

Why ‚Üí works: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí P(x)) says: IF x is in A, THEN P(x). For x ‚ąČ A,
the implication is vacuously true (we don't care about them). Only x ‚ąą A are checked.

Counter-example showing ‚ąß wrong:
  ‚ąÄn‚ąą{2,4}(n < 10) over D=‚Ąē.
  Correct expansion: ‚ąÄn(n‚ąą{2,4} ‚Üí n<10). TRUE (only n=2,4 are checked; both <10).
  Wrong expansion: ‚ąÄn(n‚ąą{2,4} ‚ąß n<10). FALSE: n=1‚ąąD but 1‚ąČ{2,4} ‚Üí conjunct fails."

Exit gate: "What connective expands bounded ‚ąÄ? Write ‚ąÄx‚ąąC P(x) in unbounded form."
Expected: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąC ‚Üí P(x)).
‚Üí Correct: return to TA-A01 P49 re-attempt.
‚Üí Incorrect: repeat the counter-example above with student's own small set.
```

### Repair Chain B-2: BOUNDED-EXISTENTIAL-WRONG-CONNECTIVE (MC-2)

```
P41 ‚ÄĒ MISCONCEPTION DETECTOR
Diagnostic: "Expand ‚ąÉx‚ąą{3,5}(x is even)."
If student writes ‚ąÉx(x‚ąą{3,5} ‚Üí x is even): MC-2 confirmed.

P27 ‚ÄĒ MISCONCEPTION NAMING
"BOUNDED-EXISTENTIAL-WRONG-CONNECTIVE: using ‚Üí for bounded ‚ąÉ.
‚ąÉx(x‚ąąA ‚Üí P(x)) is trivially TRUE whenever ‚ąÉx‚ąČA ‚ÄĒ take any x outside A,
  the implication is False‚Üíanything=True. Useless.
Correct: ‚ąÉx(x‚ąąA ‚ąß P(x)). Both conditions must be SIMULTANEOUSLY true:
  x must be IN A, AND x must satisfy P.

‚ąÉx‚ąą{3,5}(x is even):
  Wrong: ‚ąÉx(x‚ąą{3,5} ‚Üí x is even). Take x=2: 2‚ąČ{3,5} ‚Üí True. Trivially TRUE. Wrong!
  Right: ‚ąÉx(x‚ąą{3,5} ‚ąß x is even). Check 3: 3‚ąą{3,5} ‚úď BUT 3 is NOT even ‚úó.
         Check 5: 5‚ąą{3,5} ‚úď BUT 5 is NOT even ‚úó. No witness. FALSE. Correct!"

Exit gate: "What connective expands bounded ‚ąÉ? Write ‚ąÉx‚ąąC P(x) in unbounded form."
Expected: ‚ąÉx(x‚ąąC ‚ąß P(x)).
‚Üí Correct: return to TA-A01.
```

### Repair Chain B-3: UNIQUENESS-IS-EXISTENCE (MC-3)

```
P64 ‚ÄĒ CONCEPTUAL SHIFT
"BEFORE: ‚ąÉ!x P(x) says the same as ‚ąÉx P(x).
 AFTER:  ‚ąÉ!x P(x) requires EXACTLY ONE satisfier ‚ÄĒ neither zero nor two.

Three cases:
  Zero satisfiers ‚Üí ‚ąÉx P(x) is FALSE ‚Üí ‚ąÉ!x P(x) is FALSE.
  One satisfier ‚Üí ‚ąÉx P(x) is TRUE ‚Üí ‚ąÉ!x P(x) is TRUE.
  Two or more satisfiers ‚Üí ‚ąÉx P(x) is TRUE ‚Üí ‚ąÉ!x P(x) is FALSE.

‚ąÉ!x(x¬≤=4) over ‚ĄĚ: x=2 and x=‚ąí2 both satisfy ‚Üí TWO satisfiers ‚Üí ‚ąÉ!x is FALSE.
‚ąÉ!x(x¬≥=8) over ‚ĄĚ: only x=2 satisfies ‚Üí ONE satisfier ‚Üí ‚ąÉ!x is TRUE."

Exit gate: "How many satisfiers are needed for ‚ąÉ!x P(x) to be TRUE? How many for ‚ąÉx P(x)?"
Expected: exactly 1 for ‚ąÉ!; at least 1 for ‚ąÉ.
‚Üí Correct: return to TA-A02.
```

---


## Component 6 ‚ÄĒ P89 Spaced Repetition Schedule

```
P89 ‚ÄĒ SPACED REPETITION
Review schedule for math.found.quantifiers:

Interval 1 (1 day after mastery):
  Recall prompt: "State the bounded ‚ąÄ and ‚ąÉ expansion laws with their connectives.
  Expand ‚ąÄx‚ąą‚Ąē(x > 0) and ‚ąÉx‚ąą‚Ąē(x > 100) in unbounded form."
  Target: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąą‚Ąē ‚Üí x>0); ‚ąÉx(x‚ąą‚Ąē ‚ąß x>100).

Interval 2 (3 days):
  Application: "A = {n‚ąą‚Ą§ : ‚ąí5 ‚Č§ n ‚Č§ 5}.
  (a) Is ‚ąÄn‚ąąA(n¬≤ ‚Č§ 25) TRUE?  (b) Is ‚ąÉ!n‚ąąA(n¬≥=0) TRUE?"
  Target: (a) TRUE (|n|‚Č§5 ‚Üí n¬≤‚Č§25); (b) TRUE (only n=0 in A satisfies n¬≥=0).

Interval 3 (7 days):
  Transfer: "Express A ‚äÜ B ‚ąß B ‚äÜ A as a single quantifier statement.
  What does this mean about sets A and B?"
  Target: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚ÜĒ x‚ąąB), meaning A = B (same elements).

Interval 4 (21 days):
  Integration: "In a proof that A‚ą©B ‚äÜ A, write the formal statement to prove
  and describe the proof strategy (what do you assume, what do you show)."
  Target: Prove ‚ąÄx‚ąąA‚ą©B(x‚ąąA). Let x‚ąąA‚ą©B (arbitrary); then x‚ąąA ‚ąß x‚ąąB;
    in particular x‚ąąA. Done ‚ÄĒ universal proof by arbitrary element.
```

---


## Component 7 ‚ÄĒ Cross-Blueprint Dependencies

### GR-8: Cross-link documentation
```
Incoming prerequisites:
  math.found.predicate ‚Üí Defines predicates P(x) as sentence templates.
    Bounded quantifiers require predicates to be applied within a named set.
  math.found.predicate-logic ‚Üí Provides unrestricted ‚ąÄx P(x), ‚ąÉx P(x);
    negation laws ¬¨(‚ąÄ)‚Č°‚ąÉ¬¨, ¬¨(‚ąÉ)‚Č°‚ąÄ¬¨; nested quantifiers.
    Quantifiers extends this to bounded notation and the uniqueness quantifier.

Outgoing unlocks:
  math.found.proof ‚Üí Proof strategies are:
    ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Üí arbitrary-element proof (let x‚ąąA be arbitrary; prove P(x)).
    ‚ąÉx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Üí witness proof (exhibit specific a‚ąąA with P(a)).
    ‚ąÉ!x P(x) ‚Üí existence + uniqueness (prove both ‚ąÉx and the only-one property).
  math.found.set-theory ‚Üí All set operations are bounded quantifier statements.
    A‚äÜB: ‚ąÄx‚ąąA(x‚ąąB). A‚ą©B: {x:x‚ąąA‚ąßx‚ąąB}. Complement: {x‚ąąU:x‚ąČA}.

Cross-linked:
  math.found.set-theory (Tier 1) ‚Üí P76 probe: express A‚äÜB, A‚ą©B, A^c in
    bounded quantifier notation; verify on concrete sets.
```

### GR-9: P76 Mode Determination
```
cross_links = [math.found.set-theory]
math.found.set-theory IS in Tier 1 set ‚Üí P76 CROSS-LINK PROBE
P76 probe: express A‚äÜB, A‚ą©B, A^c in bounded quantifier notation;
  verify on A={2,3,5}, B={1,2,3,4}, D={1,...,5}.
  Bridges quantifiers to set-theory language.
```

---


## Component 8 ‚ÄĒ Teaching Notes

### Pedagogical Rationale
Quantifiers (bloom=understand) is a short but high-leverage concept. The entire power of formal mathematics rests on using ‚ąÄ and ‚ąÉ correctly with the right connectives. The connective asymmetry (‚Üí for bounded ‚ąÄ, ‚ąß for bounded ‚ąÉ) is non-intuitive and must be derived from first principles, not memorised as two separate rules.

### Common Session Failure Modes
1. **MC-1 and MC-2 are mirror images:** Students who learn MC-1 repair (‚ąÄ uses ‚Üí) often over-apply it and start using ‚Üí for ‚ąÉ as well (creating MC-2). Both repair chains must clarify WHY each connective was chosen, not just WHICH connective.
2. **Short session at h=4:** This blueprint covers three focused topics in fewer TAs than typical. Keep TA-A03 (set-theory bridge) brisk ‚ÄĒ it is preparation for the P76 probe and the downstream math.found.set-theory concept.
3. **‚ąÉ! requires two sub-proofs:** Students encounter ‚ąÉ! in proofs later (math.found.proof) and must know: (a) prove existence, (b) prove uniqueness separately. The formal expansion ‚ąÉx(P(x) ‚ąß ‚ąÄy(P(y)‚Üíy=x)) encodes both.

### Connections to Broader Curriculum
- **Downstream ‚ÄĒ math.found.proof:** The proof structure for ‚ąÄ (arbitrary element) and ‚ąÉ (exhibit witness) are direct translations of the semantics of quantifiers.
- **Downstream ‚ÄĒ math.found.set-theory:** Every set operation has a quantifier encoding; fluency here makes set-theory proofs transparent.
- **Predecessor ‚ÄĒ math.found.predicate-logic:** Predicate-logic introduced ‚ąÄ/‚ąÉ unrestricted; quantifiers deepens into bounded forms and uniqueness.

### Language Precision
- "Bounded universal" for ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x); "bounded existential" for ‚ąÉx‚ąąA P(x).
- Always say which connective: "bounded ‚ąÄ uses implication; bounded ‚ąÉ uses conjunction."
- "Uniqueness quantifier" for ‚ąÉ! ‚ÄĒ never called "the existence quantifier."

---


## Component 10 ‚ÄĒ Validation Checklist

### Structural Validators
- [x] V-1: Blueprint has all 10 components (0‚Äď8, 10).
- [x] V-2: Metadata YAML complete with all required fields.
- [x] V-3: concept_id matches filename `math.found.quantifiers`.
- [x] V-4: difficulty=developing, bloom_level=understand, mastery_threshold=0.80.
- [x] V-5: estimated_hours=4, session_ta_cap=7.

### Grammar Rule Validators
- [x] V-6 (GR-1): Every non-repair TA opens with B-category primitive (TA-A01: P06+P03; TA-A02: P11; TA-A03: P04+P11; TA-A04 is mastery gate).
- [x] V-7 (GR-2): Every non-gate TA contains P49 with all 4 branches. TA-A01, TA-A02, TA-A03 each have P49.
- [x] V-8 (GR-3): Mastery gate TA-A04 is terminal (ends with P78).
- [x] V-9 (GR-4): P41 entry diagnostic gates P64 ‚Üí Protocol B when MC-1 active.
- [x] V-10 (GR-6): P91 compound (P77‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP76‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP75‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP74‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP78) complete in TA-A04.
- [x] V-11 (GR-7): P76 present in mastery gate TA-A04.
- [x] V-12 (GR-8): Cross-links documented in Component 7. math.found.set-theory named.
- [x] V-13 (GR-9): P76 mode is CROSS-LINK (math.found.set-theory is Tier 1). P76 probe: A‚äÜB, A‚ą©B, A^c in bounded quantifier notation on concrete sets. Bridges to set-theory.
- [x] V-14 (GR-10): MAMR stated in Component 0 and enforced in Component 3.

### Content Validators
- [x] V-15: Pass criterion = ‚Ćą0.80 √ó 5‚ĆČ = 4/5. P77 has 4 problems; P76 contributes 1; total n=5. ‚úď
- [x] V-16: CPA_ENTRY_STAGE = C. developing always starts Concrete. ‚úď
- [x] V-17: MC-1 is FOUNDATIONAL and cleared first per MAMR. ‚úď
- [x] V-18: P49 INCORRECT branch activates P27 or P64 with repair pathway in all three non-gate TAs. ‚úď
- [x] V-19: P76 probe independent of P77 problems (P77: expansion/verification on finite sets, negation, discriminant equation; P76: set operations as bounded quantifiers on distinct A,B ‚ÄĒ different). ‚úď
- [x] V-20: Spaced repetition intervals are 1, 3, 7, 21 days with distinct prompts. ‚úď

### Automated Invariant Check (AIR)
- [x] AIR-1: No implementation code present.
- [x] AIR-2: No references to internal system variables.
- [x] AIR-3: All cross-linked concept IDs use canonical prefix format.
- [x] AIR-4: P91 sequence order is P77‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP76‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP75‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP74‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP78. ‚úď
- [x] AIR-5: Protocol B repair chains each have explicit exit gate with return instruction. ‚úď

**VALIDATION RESULT: PASS ‚ÄĒ PACKAGE_READY**

assets.title: Scaffolding Protocol
assets.content:
### Entry Diagnostic (P41 gate)
```
P41 ‚ÄĒ MISCONCEPTION DETECTOR
Prompt: "Domain D = ‚Ą§. Set A = {2, 4, 6}.
Expand ‚ąÄx‚ąąA (x < 10) into an unbounded form using D.
Which logical connective do you use: ‚Üí or ‚ąß?"

CORRECT (‚ąÄx(x‚ąą{2,4,6} ‚Üí x < 10); uses ‚Üí):
‚Üí No MC-1 detected ‚Üí proceed to TA-A01 standard opening.

INCORRECT (writes ‚ąÄx(x‚ąą{2,4,6} ‚ąß x < 10); uses ‚ąß):
‚Üí MC-1 FOUNDATIONAL active ‚Üí P64 CONCEPTUAL SHIFT
‚Üí Activate Protocol B (MC-1 Repair Chain)
```

### Prerequisite Bridge
Student completed math.found.predicate-logic (predicates, unrestricted ‚ąÄx P(x) and ‚ąÉx P(x), negation laws, nested quantifiers). Quantifiers deepens this by introducing BOUNDED quantification ‚ÄĒ restricting the domain to a named set A ‚ÄĒ and the uniqueness quantifier ‚ąÉ!.

### MAMR Enforcement
MC-1 is FOUNDATIONAL. The wrong connective for bounded ‚ąÄ corrupts the formal expansion into a false over-constraining statement. Clear completely before discussing ‚ąÉ connectives (MC-2) or ‚ąÉ! (MC-3).

### CPA Progression: C ‚Üí P ‚Üí A
- **Concrete (C):** Small finite A={2,4,6}; enumerate to verify truth of ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) and ‚ąÉx‚ąąA P(x).
- **Pictorial (P):** Venn diagram with domain D as outer rectangle, A as inner circle; ‚ąÄx‚ąąA checks only inside circle.
- **Abstract (A):** Formal expansion laws; counterexample analysis; set-theory encoding.

---

assets.title: P89 Spaced Repetition Schedule
assets.content:
```
P89 ‚ÄĒ SPACED REPETITION
Review schedule for math.found.quantifiers:

Interval 1 (1 day after mastery):
  Recall prompt: "State the bounded ‚ąÄ and ‚ąÉ expansion laws with their connectives.
  Expand ‚ąÄx‚ąą‚Ąē(x > 0) and ‚ąÉx‚ąą‚Ąē(x > 100) in unbounded form."
  Target: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąą‚Ąē ‚Üí x>0); ‚ąÉx(x‚ąą‚Ąē ‚ąß x>100).

Interval 2 (3 days):
  Application: "A = {n‚ąą‚Ą§ : ‚ąí5 ‚Č§ n ‚Č§ 5}.
  (a) Is ‚ąÄn‚ąąA(n¬≤ ‚Č§ 25) TRUE?  (b) Is ‚ąÉ!n‚ąąA(n¬≥=0) TRUE?"
  Target: (a) TRUE (|n|‚Č§5 ‚Üí n¬≤‚Č§25); (b) TRUE (only n=0 in A satisfies n¬≥=0).

Interval 3 (7 days):
  Transfer: "Express A ‚äÜ B ‚ąß B ‚äÜ A as a single quantifier statement.
  What does this mean about sets A and B?"
  Target: ‚ąÄx(x‚ąąA ‚ÜĒ x‚ąąB), meaning A = B (same elements).

Interval 4 (21 days):
  Integration: "In a proof that A‚ą©B ‚äÜ A, write the formal statement to prove
  and describe the proof strategy (what do you assume, what do you show)."
  Target: Prove ‚ąÄx‚ąąA‚ą©B(x‚ąąA). Let x‚ąąA‚ą©B (arbitrary); then x‚ąąA ‚ąß x‚ąąB;
    in particular x‚ąąA. Done ‚ÄĒ universal proof by arbitrary element.
```

---

assets.title: Cross-Blueprint Dependencies
assets.content:
### GR-8: Cross-link documentation
```
Incoming prerequisites:
  math.found.predicate ‚Üí Defines predicates P(x) as sentence templates.
    Bounded quantifiers require predicates to be applied within a named set.
  math.found.predicate-logic ‚Üí Provides unrestricted ‚ąÄx P(x), ‚ąÉx P(x);
    negation laws ¬¨(‚ąÄ)‚Č°‚ąÉ¬¨, ¬¨(‚ąÉ)‚Č°‚ąÄ¬¨; nested quantifiers.
    Quantifiers extends this to bounded notation and the uniqueness quantifier.

Outgoing unlocks:
  math.found.proof ‚Üí Proof strategies are:
    ‚ąÄx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Üí arbitrary-element proof (let x‚ąąA be arbitrary; prove P(x)).
    ‚ąÉx‚ąąA P(x) ‚Üí witness proof (exhibit specific a‚ąąA with P(a)).
    ‚ąÉ!x P(x) ‚Üí existence + uniqueness (prove both ‚ąÉx and the only-one property).
  math.found.set-theory ‚Üí All set operations are bounded quantifier statements.
    A‚äÜB: ‚ąÄx‚ąąA(x‚ąąB). A‚ą©B: {x:x‚ąąA‚ąßx‚ąąB}. Complement: {x‚ąąU:x‚ąČA}.

Cross-linked:
  math.found.set-theory (Tier 1) ‚Üí P76 probe: express A‚äÜB, A‚ą©B, A^c in
    bounded quantifier notation; verify on concrete sets.
```

### GR-9: P76 Mode Determination
```
cross_links = [math.found.set-theory]
math.found.set-theory IS in Tier 1 set ‚Üí P76 CROSS-LINK PROBE
P76 probe: express A‚äÜB, A‚ą©B, A^c in bounded quantifier notation;
  verify on A={2,3,5}, B={1,2,3,4}, D={1,...,5}.
  Bridges quantifiers to set-theory language.
```
